## (a) Implementation

```dafny
function Canonicalize(x: Id): Id
{
  if x.width >= PAD then x
  else Id(x.kind, x.value, PAD)
}
```

## (b) Lemma specification

```dafny
lemma Spec(x: Id)
  requires Wellformed(x)
  ensures
    // kind and value are never changed
    Canonicalize(x).kind == x.kind &&
    Canonicalize(x).value == x.value &&
    // result is canonical width: at least PAD
    Canonicalize(x).width >= PAD &&
    // result is exactly PAD unless the input already needed more
    Canonicalize(x).width == (if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD) &&
    // result is wellformed
    Wellformed(Canonicalize(x)) &&
    // already-canonical ids are returned unchanged (no-op)
    (x.width >= PAD ==> Canonicalize(x) == x) &&
    // canonicalization is idempotent
    Canonicalize(Canonicalize(x)) == Canonicalize(x)
{ }
```

### Why this is complete

- **Kind/value invariance**: `kind` and `value` must never change — pinned to `x.kind`, `x.value`.
- **Minimum width 4**: result width `>= PAD`.
- **Exact width**: the result width is `max(x.width, PAD)`, captured by the conditional clause. This forbids over-padding (e.g. padding to 5) and forbids shrinking a wide id.
- **No-op on already-canonical**: when `x.width >= PAD`, result equals `x` exactly (this combined with the width clause fully pins the wide case).
- **Wellformedness preserved**: result still satisfies `Wellformed`.
- **Idempotence**: canonicalizing twice equals canonicalizing once.

The exact-width clause `Canonicalize(x).width == (if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD)` together with kind/value fixing actually determines the result uniquely, so the other clauses are derivable consequences included for explicitness and audit clarity.
